---
title: "Succession: Viewership Report"
author: "Artemiy Yashin 358561"
date: "4-16-2026"
format: html
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
  message: false
---

# Succession

*Succession* is an American satirical comedy-drama television series created by Jesse Armstrong. It centers on the Roy family, the dysfunctional owners of Waystar RoyCo, a global media and entertainment conglomerate. The series follows the family members as they fight for control of the company amid uncertainty about the health of the family's patriarch, *Logan Roy*.

![Succession Title Card](images/succession.png)

## Viewership Statistics

The show garnered significant critical acclaim and a dedicated audience over its four-season run on HBO. Below is a summary of the average US viewership for each season.

```{r}
#| label: load-data

library(ggplot2)
library(knitr)

# Average US viewers in millions (Data sourced from Wikipedia)
season <- c(1, 2, 3, 4)
viewers <- c(0.603, 0.597, 0.553, 0.705)
succession_data <- data.frame(Season = season, Viewers = viewers)

# Calculate season-to-season change
succession_data$Change <- c(NA, diff(succession_data$Viewers))

# Display as a clean table
kable(succession_data, 
      col.names = c("Season", "Average Viewers (Millions)", "Change (Millions)"), 
      caption = "Succession Viewership Summary")
#| label: fig-viewership
#| fig-cap: "Average US viewership per season for Succession."
```

## Viewership Over Time

```{r}
ggplot(succession_data, aes(x = Season, y = Viewers)) +
  geom_line(color = "#4A90E2", linewidth = 1) +
  geom_point(color = "#1C2833", size = 3) +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(x = "Season", y = "Average Viewers (Millions)") +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = 1:4)
```

## Season-to-Season Changes

```{r}
#| label: fig-changes
#| fig-cap: "Change in average viewership compared to the previous season."

# Filter out the first season since it has no prior season to compare to
change_data <- succession_data[!is.na(succession_data$Change), ]

ggplot(change_data, aes(x = factor(Season), y = Change, fill = Change > 0)) +
  geom_col() +
  theme_minimal() +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("TRUE" = "#2ECC71", "FALSE" = "#E74C3C")) +
  labs(x = "Season", y = "Change in Viewers (Millions)") +
  theme(legend.position = "none")
```

## Observed Changes

```{r}
#| label: calc-changes
# Save the specific change between Season 3 and 4 to a variable for inline text
change_s3_to_s4 <- succession_data$Change[4]
```

As seen in the data, the viewership experienced a slight dip during the middle seasons. However, the show saw a massive resurgence in its final season. Most notably, the viewership increased by r change_s3_to_s4 million viewers between Season 3 and Season 4, culminating in the show's highest average ratings.
